segments.s073-g25: هوا را uses را as the definite direct-object marker (the air = the thing affected). به شمشیر is an instrumental prepositional phrase (‘with/by the sword’) where به has the instrumental meaning. گریـان کند is the verbal construction: گریـان is the participial/adjectival stem from گریاندن (to cause to weep) and کند is the 3rd-person singular present/past-poetic auxiliary ‘does/makes’, so together ‘make (the air) weep’.
segments.s073-g26: بر آتش is a locative/prepositional phrase with بر = ‘on/upon’ (on the fire). یکی is an indefinite subject meaning ‘one/someone’ placed before the object. گور here is the noun being roasted and takes the less common, older sense ‘onager’ (wild ass). بریان کند is the verbal phrase where بریان (roasted) is used with کردن/کند to mean ‘roast (something)’, with کند as the 3rd-person verb.
segments.s073-g27: کمر بسته is a fixed adjectival phrase meaning ‘girded’ or ‘with belts/girdles tied’ (literally ‘waist tied’). شهریاران is the subject ‘the kings’ and بود is the past copula ‘were’; the order places the predicate phrase before the subject with بود closing the clause (poetic word order).
segments.s073-g28: به ایران is a beneficiary/goal phrase ‘for/to Iran’ with به = ‘for/to’. پناه is a predicate noun ‘refuge/protection’ and سواران is the subject ‘the horsemen/riders’; بود is again the past copula ‘were’. The line therefore reads ‘the horsemen were a refuge for Iran’ (agent/subject after the predicate is common in poetic Persian).
segments.s073-g1: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he ordered/declared.' تا here introduces the subordinate/purpose clause triggered by that command ('that / so that ...'). موبدان و ردان are a coordinated noun phrase listing groups called to the assembly; موبدان is the plural of mōbed ('Zoroastrian priests'), while ردان is another group-name (uncertain/archaic here) joined by و 'and'.
segments.s073-g2: ستاره‌شناسان is a plural noun 'astronomers/astrologers' and continues the list introduced before. و 'and' simply joins items; هم 'also/likewise' emphasizes inclusion; بخردان is a plural adjective/noun 'the wise, the prudent' (here used as a group name). Together this segment completes the enumeration of people ordered to assemble.
segments.s073-g3: کنند is the plural subjunctive/optative form 'they should do' (here completing the verb started by بفرمود تا). انجمن is 'assembly/meeting.' پیش تخت بلند literally 'before the high/lofty throne' — پیش + تخت means 'in the presence of / before the throne' and بلند modifies تخت ('lofty/high'). The word order (verb before subject phrase) is typical when the verb is placed at the start of the subordinate clause introduced by تا.
segments.s073-g4: به کار سپهری پژوهش کنند is a purpose clause: به کار 'concerning the work/affairs', سپهری qualifies it as 'celestial/heavenly', پژوهش کنند is 'they should research/investigate.' Here به takes the sense 'in/into/regarding' and the verb کنَند repeats the subjunctive 'they should do' to mark the intended activity.
segments.s073-g5: برفتند is simple past 'they went/off they departed.' و 'and' links the next clause. بردند رنج دراز literally 'they bore/took long hardship' — بردند (they carried/bore) + رنج دراز ('long suffering/hardship'), an idiom meaning 'they endured a long toil.'
segments.s073-g6: که تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/explanation ('in order that / so that'), with که as a linker 'that' and تا giving a purposive nuance. با ستاره چه دارند راز is an object-clause: با ستاره 'with the star(s)' (i.e. concerning the heavens), چه 'what', دارند 'they have/there are' and راز 'secret.' Grammatically this asks 'what secret is with the stars' (i.e. what secrets the stars hold); دارند is present/plural 'have.'
segments.s073-g7: سه روز 'three days' gives the time span. اندرَان ('اندران') here means 'in that/therein' or 'among them,' so the phrase means 'for three days there was (a) pause in their work.' کارشان 'their work' (کار + شان) and شد درنگ 'there came a delay' — شد is past 'became/happened' and درنگ is 'a pause/delay.'
segments.s073-g8: برفتند 'they departed/went off.' با زیج رومی 'with the Roman zij' — زیج are astronomical tables/handbooks and رومی qualifies them as Byzantine/Rûmian. به چنگ is an idiom meaning 'in hand' or 'in their grasp' (به + چنگ, literally 'to/claw,' here figurative). The whole clause says they left carrying the Roman zij.
segments.s073-g9: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted clause: 'زبان بر گشادند بر شهریار' literally 'tongues toward opened to the king.' گشادند is an older/past plural verb (classical form of 'گشودند') meaning 'opened' and takes 'زبان' (speech/utterances) as the thing opened. The preposition بر before شهریار marks direction/target ('to/toward the king'); the whole phrase means 'they opened their mouths to the king' (i.e., addressed the king).
segments.s073-g10: که introduces reported content ('saying that...'). کردیم is a past plural 'we did / we have done' used here to mean 'we reckoned / we counted.' با is the instrumental 'by/with', and چرخ گردان is a compound where چرخ 'the celestial sphere / heaven' is qualified by گردان 'revolving/turning' (an adjectival/epithet). شمار is the object 'count/numbering.' Together: 'that we counted by the revolving heavens.'
segments.s073-g11: چنین آمد is a stock poetic opener 'thus came' (introducing an appearance). از داد here means 'from (divine/rectifying) justice'—داد is the abstract noun 'justice' not the verb 'gave'—and اختر پدید 'a star appeared/manifested': پدید is the verb 'to appear/manifest' placed after اختر in the classical word order.
segments.s073-g12: که introduces the clause quoted or prophesied ('that...'). این آب روشن is the noun phrase 'this bright/clear water.' بخواهد is the present/future auxiliary meaning 'will / is going to' (classical use of خواستن as future modal), and دوید is the verb 'to run/flow' used here of water: 'will flow/rush forth.'
segments.s073-g13: ازین is the fused poetic form of 'از این' ('from this'); دخت مهراب = 'the daughter of Mehrab' (Mahrab as a proper name); و از پور سام = 'and from the son/descendant of Sam.' پور is the noun 'son / descendant' used to indicate parentage. The whole phrase lists the two lineages that will be parents.
segments.s073-g14: گوی here is a poetic noun used as a kenning for the coming person/being (literally 'sphere' / 'orb' but used metaphorically); پر منش is an adjectival phrase where پر 'full (of)' modifies منش 'character / origin' — 'full of noble character / lineage.' زاید is the verb 'to be born / to arise' (classical present/future sense 'shall be born'). و نیک‌نام appends a second predicative quality: 'and well‑named / of good repute.'
segments.s073-g15: بود is the past copula 'was'; زندگانیش is a noun phrase 'his life' (زندگی / زنده‌گی with possessive -ش). بسیار here quantifies that life ('long / many'); مر is the dative/enclitic meaning 'for me' (poetic order places it after the quantity). The clause means 'his life was long for me' (i.e., he lived many years while I was concerned).
segments.s073-g16: همش literally 'all of it' or 'entirely'; زور here is 'strength / might.' باشد is an optative/subjunctive third person 'may it be / may it remain' (a wish or blessing). هم links 'also' and the final two nouns آیین 'custom/ordinance' and فر 'royal glory' are added as things the speaker wishes to endure. The phrase functions as a brief blessing: 'may all his strength remain, and likewise his custom and glory.'
segments.s073-g17: This clause strings adjectival and nominal descriptions together: همش (indefinite 'all of it') + برز (adjective 'high/exalted') + باشد (copular/be verb). The second part repeats همش + شاخ و یال (two coordinated nouns 'horn and mane') to give parallel descriptive images. 'باشد' is an archaic/poetic present/subjunctive form of بودن used here as a simple copula ('is'). Word order is topic/descriptor then verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s073-g18: به رزم و به بزمش is a paired prepositional phrase ('in/at battle and at his feast'); نباشد (negative of باشد) states nonexistence of a counterpart. Here نباشد functions as a negative copula ('there is not' / 'he has no'). همال is the noun asserted to be absent (i.e. 'a peer/equal'); the clause therefore means 'he has no equal in war or in feasting.'
segments.s073-g19: کجا باره او کند is a temporal/conditional clause ('whenever he takes up a charge / on every occasion he makes'), with کجا used idiomatically as 'when/whenever.' او is the subject and کند is the verb 'does/makes.' موی‌تر is a comparative/derived adjective from موی ('hair') meaning 'more-haired' or 'hair-bristling' and is the result-state tied to that occasion (موی‌تر شود in the next clause).
segments.s073-g20: This clause has the verb شود ('become') + adjective خشک ('dry') to state a change of state: 'becomes dry.' همرزم او را is the direct object phrase ('his comrade' marked by را), so the clause reads 'his comrade's jigar/heart becomes dry.' Jigar here is used idiomatically for the seat of feeling/courage rather than the literal liver.
segments.s073-g21: The clause starts with the subject عقاب ('the eagle') followed by a prepositional structure از بر ترگ او ('from/on the shred of him') and the negative verb نگذرد ('does not pass'). نگذرد is a present/future-oriented negative of گذشتن used poetically to say 'will not pass / will not let pass.' The phrase means roughly 'an eagle will not pass by even a shred of him.'
segments.s073-g22: This clause has سران جهان را (the chiefs/leaders of the world, object-marked) followed by بکس نشمرد. نشمرد is the negative past/present of شمردن used here as 'did not count/consider'; بکس is a generic indefinite ('anybody' / 'a person') in classical usage. Together the clause means 'he did not count the world's chiefs as anyone (of consequence).' Word order places the object/topic first, then the verbal predicate.
segments.s073-g23: یکی introduces an indefinite subject ('a certain one'). برز بالا is a compound adjectival description ('high, lofty') and بود is the past copula 'was.' فرمند is an archaic/poetic past-tense verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (parallel to فرمود). Together this establishes 'There was a certain lofty one who commanded...'.
segments.s073-g24: همه شیر گیرد uses گیرد (present/subjunctive or jussive of گرفتن) to express a commanded or general action: 'let all the lions be taken' or 'all the lions seize.' به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase ('into the bend/loop of the snare') specifying the place/means. Word order is verb-final in the predicate, typical of Persian poetry, with the prepositional phrase marking the goal.

sentences.s073-s13: He makes the air weep with his sword; upon the fire someone roasts an onager.
sentences.s073-s14: The kings were girded; the horsemen were the refuge of Iran.
sentences.s073-s1: He commanded that the mobeds, the radān, the astronomers, and likewise the wise (be gathered)...
sentences.s073-s2: —to hold an assembly before the lofty throne and to investigate matters of the heavens.
sentences.s073-s3: They went off and suffered long hardship, on purpose to learn what secret the stars hold.
sentences.s073-s4: For three days there came a pause in their work; then they set out with the Roman zij in hand.
sentences.s073-s5: They opened their mouths to the king, saying that we have counted by the revolving heavens.
sentences.s073-s6: Thus from Justice a star appeared, and it proclaimed that this clear water will flow.
sentences.s073-s7: From this daughter of Mehrab and from the son of Sam shall be born one of noble disposition and good repute.
sentences.s073-s8: His life was long for me; may all his strength remain, and his custom and glory as well.
sentences.s073-s9: All of it is lofty, all of it horn and mane; in battle and in feast he has no equal.
sentences.s073-s10: Whenever he takes up his burden his hair bristles; his comrade's heart grows dry.
sentences.s073-s11: An eagle would not pass over even a shred of him; he did not reckon the world's chiefs as anyone.
sentences.s073-s12: There was a certain lofty one, a commander; he ordered: 'Let every lion be taken into the curve of the snare.'
